target.name: N-Me-carbazoquinocin A
target.motivation: Carbazole alkaloids, particularly carbazoquinocins, exhibit strong inhibitory activity against lipid peroxidation. This work aims to extend a previously developed methodology for the total synthesis of N-Me-analogues of five members of the carbazoquinocin family.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Total synthesis of carbazoquinocins A and D
Brønsted acid-catalyzed intramolecular benzannulation of C-3-tethered indole-propargylic alcohols for N-Me-carbazoquinocin C, N-Me-carazostatin and N-Me-lipocarbazole A4

success_criteria.free_text: Efficient and practically viable synthetic approach for the unified synthesis of 3,4-carbazoquinone-based carbazole alkaloids N-Me-carbazoquinocins A, B and D–F.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond formation to introduce alkyl chain at C1 position
key_disconnections.reasoning: To introduce the required alkyl chain at the C1 position of the carbazole framework.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-Br bond formation at C3 position
key_disconnections.reasoning: To introduce a handle for subsequent methoxylation.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-O bond formation at C3 position
key_disconnections.reasoning: To convert the bromine into a methoxy group.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-O bond cleavage at C3 position
key_disconnections.reasoning: To deprotect the methoxy group to a phenol.

key_disconnections.bond_description: Oxidation of C3 and C4 positions

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The overall strategy involves constructing the carbazole framework using a previously developed acid-catalyzed intramolecular benzannulation. The alkyl chain at the C1 position is introduced via Wittig olefination. Subsequent functionalization at the C3 position (bromination, methoxylation, demethylation) and final oxidation leads to the carbazoquinone natural products.

thought.background: Carbazole alkaloids, particularly carbazoquinocins, are known for their bioactivity, specifically strong inhibitory activity against lipid peroxidation. Several total syntheses of carbazoquinocins A-F have been reported.
thought.gap: A unified and efficient synthetic approach for N-Me-analogues of multiple carbazoquinocins (A, B, D-F) is desired, building upon a recently developed intramolecular benzannulation strategy.
thought.decision: To extend the previously developed acid-catalyzed intramolecular benzannulation of C-3-tethered indole-propargylic alcohols for the construction of the carbazole unit, and then functionalize it to obtain N-Me-carbazoquinocins A, B, and D–F.

outcome.observation: Identified a promising strategy for the synthesis of N-Me-carbazoquinocin B, D, E, F, and A.

evidence.text_span: Recently, in 2022, we developed an approach for the total synthesis of the 1-alkyl-2-methyl-3,4-carbazoquinone natural product N-Me-carbazoquinocin C together with N-Me-carazostatin and N-Me-lipocarbazole A4.7d This approach employs a Brønsted acid-catalyzed intramolecular benzannulation of C-3-tethered indole-propargylic alcohols (1) (Scheme 1A) as the key step for the construction of a carbazole unit (2). In continuation, we envisioned an opportunity to extend this methodology for the total synthesis of other members of the carbazoquinocin family, i.e., carbazoquinocin A, B, and D–F (3–7) (Fig. 1).

thought.background: The carbazole–butyraldehyde (2) is a known intermediate from previous work.
thought.gap: The next step is to introduce the specific alkyl chain for N-Me-carbazoquinocin B via Wittig olefination.
thought.decision: To synthesize olefin (11a) by Wittig olefination of aldehyde 2 with isopropyltriphenylphosphonium bromide (10a) and n-BuLi, expecting the formation of the desired olefin.

outcome.observation: Olefin (11a) was obtained in 76% yield as a colorless gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: Accordingly, Wittig olefination of aldehyde 2 with phosphorous ylide (9a) at 0 °C (generated in situ from (iso-propyl)PPh3Br8 (10a) and n BuLi at 0 °C) afforded olefin (11a) in 76% yield.

thought.gap: The double bond needs to be reduced to a saturated alkyl chain.
thought.decision: To perform hydrogenation of 11a using Pd/C under a hydrogen atmosphere, expecting the formation of 1-isoheptylcarbazole (12a).

outcome.observation: 1-isoheptylcarbazole (12a) was obtained in 96% yield as a gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: The hydrogenation reaction of 11a with Pd/C (10%) under an H2 atmosphere in ethyl acetate at room temperature (rt) for 6 h gave 1-isoheptylcarbazole (12a) (96% yield).

thought.gap: A bromine atom needs to be introduced at the C3 position for further functionalization.
thought.decision: To perform regioselective electrophilic bromination of 12a with NBS in CHCl3, expecting the formation of 3-bromocarbazole (13a).

outcome.observation: 3-bromocarbazole (13a) was obtained in 90% yield as a white solid.

evidence.text_span: Next, the regioselective electrophilic bromination of carbazole 12a with NBS in CHCl3 at rt gave the corresponding 3- bromocarbazole (13a) (90%) within 6 min reaction time.

thought.gap: The bromine needs to be replaced by a methoxy group.
thought.decision: To perform methoxylation of 13a using NaOMe and CuI in DMF, expecting the formation of 3-methoxy carbazole (14a).

outcome.observation: 3-methoxy carbazole (14a) was obtained in 90% yield as a colorless solid.

evidence.text_span: Initially, heating a DMF suspension of 13a, NaOMe and CuI at 115 °C gave 3-methoxy carbazole (14a) in 90% yield.

thought.gap: The methoxy group needs to be deprotected to a hydroxyl group.
thought.decision: To perform demethylation of 14a using BBr3 in CH2Cl2, expecting the formation of phenol product (15a).

outcome.observation: Phenol product (15a) was obtained in 82% yield as a pale-yellow liquid.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent treatment of 14a with BBr3 in CH2Cl2 at 0 °C to rt for 14 h resulted in the formation of the phenol product (15a) in 82% yield via deprotection of the methoxy group.

thought.gap: The final step is to oxidize the phenol to the carbazoquinone.
thought.decision: To perform oxidation of 15a using (PhSeO)2O in THF, expecting the formation of N-Me-carbazoquinocin B (3).

outcome.observation: N-Me-carbazoquinocin B (3) was obtained in 95% yield as a dark-brown glittering solid.

evidence.text_span: Finally, the synthesis of N-Me-carbazoquinocin B 3 was achieved by employing the (PhSeO)2O-promoted oxidation of 15a. Accordingly, heating (50 °C) a solution of 15a and (PhSeO)2O in THF gave N-Me-carbazoquinocin B 3 in 95% yield.

thought.background: The synthesis of N-Me-carbazoquinocin B was successful, establishing a general strategy.
thought.gap: To synthesize N-Me-carbazoquinocin D, a different alkyl chain needs to be introduced via Wittig olefination.
thought.decision: To perform Wittig olefination of aldehyde 2 using sec-butylidene phosphorous ylide (9b) (generated from (sec-butyl)PPh3Br (10b) and n-BuLi), expecting the formation of Z-olefin (11b).

outcome.observation: Z-olefin (11b) was obtained in 76% yield as a colorless liquid. The geometry was confirmed by NOESY NMR.

evidence.text_span: Therefore, olefination of aldehyde 2 using sec-butylidene phosphorous ylide 9b (generated from (sec-butyl)PPh3Br8 10b and nBuLi at 0 °C) afforded Z-olefin 11b (R = Me) in 76% yield. The geometry of the olefin in 11b was identified by using the correlations observed between ]CMe and -C-H in the corresponding NOESY NMR spectrum.

thought.gap: The double bond needs to be reduced to a saturated alkyl chain.
thought.decision: To perform hydrogenation of 11b using Pd/C under a hydrogen atmosphere, expecting the formation of 12b.

outcome.observation: Hydrogenation product 12b was obtained in 91% yield as a colorless liquid.

evidence.text_span: Further, by operating the same sequence of five functional group transformations, as depicted in Scheme 2 (R = Me), i.e., hydrogenation (12b, 91%)

thought.gap: A bromine atom needs to be introduced at the C3 position for further functionalization.
thought.decision: To perform electrophilic bromination of 12b with NBS in CHCl3, expecting the formation of 13b.

outcome.observation: Bromination product 13b was obtained in 92% yield as a gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: Further, by operating the same sequence of five functional group transformations, as depicted in Scheme 2 (R = Me), i.e., hydrogenation (12b, 91%)–bromination (13b, 92%)

thought.gap: The bromine needs to be replaced by a methoxy group.
thought.decision: To perform methoxylation of 13b using NaOMe and CuI in DMF, expecting the formation of 14b.

outcome.observation: 3-methoxycarbazole 14b was obtained in 90% yield as a colorless liquid.

evidence.text_span: Further, by operating the same sequence of five functional group transformations, as depicted in Scheme 2 (R = Me), i.e., hydrogenation (12b, 91%)–bromination (13b, 92%)–methoxylation (14b, 90%)

thought.gap: The methoxy group needs to be deprotected to a hydroxyl group.
thought.decision: To perform demethylation of 14b using BBr3 in CH2Cl2, expecting the formation of phenol 15b.

outcome.observation: Phenol 15b was obtained in 91% yield as a gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: Further, by operating the same sequence of five functional group transformations, as depicted in Scheme 2 (R = Me), i.e., hydrogenation (12b, 91%)–bromination (13b, 92%)–methoxylation (14b, 90%)–demethylation (15b, 91%)

thought.gap: The final step is to oxidize the phenol to the carbazoquinone.
thought.decision: To perform oxidation of 15b using (PhSeO)2O in THF, expecting the formation of N-Me-carbazoquinocin D (4).

outcome.observation: N-Me-carbazoquinocin D (4) was obtained in 89% yield as a dark-brown glittering solid.

evidence.text_span: Further, by operating the same sequence of five functional group transformations, as depicted in Scheme 2 (R = Me), i.e., hydrogenation (12b, 91%)–bromination (13b, 92%)–methoxylation (14b, 90%)–demethylation (15b, 91%)–oxidation (4, 89%), we efficiently converted olefin 11b into N-Mecarbazoquinocin D 4, with an overall yield of 61% for five steps.

thought.background: The general strategy for synthesizing N-Me-carbazoquinocins B and D has been established.
thought.gap: To synthesize N-Me-carbazoquinocin E, a different alkyl chain (iso-butyl) needs to be introduced via Wittig olefination.
thought.decision: To perform Wittig olefination of aldehyde 2 using iso-butylidene phosphorous ylide (9c), expecting the formation of Z-olefin (11c).

outcome.observation: Z-olefin (11c) was obtained in 81% yield as a colorless gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: Wittig olefination of aldehyde 2 using iso-butylidene phosphorous ylide 9c (ref. 8) and iso-pentylidene phosphorous ylide 9d (ref. 8) exclusively afforded Z-olefins 11c (n = 0; 81% and 11d (n = 1; 80%), respectively.

thought.gap: The double bond needs to be reduced to a saturated alkyl chain.
thought.decision: To perform hydrogenation of 11c using Pd/C under a hydrogen atmosphere, expecting the formation of 12c.

outcome.observation: Hydrogenation product 12c was obtained in 93% yield as a colorless liquid.

evidence.text_span: After having both olefins 11c and 11d in hand, we employed the five-step synthetic strategy (as shown for N-Me-carbazoquinocin B 3 and D 4 in Scheme 2), i.e., hydrogenation (12c & 12d)

thought.gap: A bromine atom needs to be introduced at the C3 position for further functionalization.
thought.decision: To perform electrophilic bromination of 12c with NBS in CHCl3, expecting the formation of 13c.

outcome.observation: Bromination product 13c was obtained in 91% yield as a gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: After having both olefins 11c and 11d in hand, we employed the five-step synthetic strategy (as shown for N-Me-carbazoquinocin B 3 and D 4 in Scheme 2), i.e., hydrogenation (12c & 12d)–bromination (13c & 13d)

thought.gap: The bromine needs to be replaced by a methoxy group.
thought.decision: To perform methoxylation of 13c using NaOMe and CuI in DMF, expecting the formation of 14c.

outcome.observation: 3-methoxycarbazole 14c was obtained in 93% yield as a yellowish liquid.

evidence.text_span: After having both olefins 11c and 11d in hand, we employed the five-step synthetic strategy (as shown for N-Me-carbazoquinocin B 3 and D 4 in Scheme 2), i.e., hydrogenation (12c & 12d)–bromination (13c & 13d)–methoxylation (14c & 14d)

thought.gap: The methoxy group needs to be deprotected to a hydroxyl group.
thought.decision: To perform demethylation of 14c using BBr3 in CH2Cl2, expecting the formation of phenol 15c.

outcome.observation: Phenol 15c was obtained in 95% yield as a white gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: After having both olefins 11c and 11d in hand, we employed the five-step synthetic strategy (as shown for N-Me-carbazoquinocin B 3 and D 4 in Scheme 2), i.e., hydrogenation (12c & 12d)–bromination (13c & 13d)–methoxylation (14c & 14d)–demethylation (15c & 15d)

thought.gap: The final step is to oxidize the phenol to the carbazoquinone.
thought.decision: To perform oxidation of 15c using (PhSeO)2O in THF, expecting the formation of N-Me-carbazoquinocin E (5).

outcome.observation: N-Me-carbazoquinocin E (5) was obtained in 91% yield as a dark-brown glittering solid.

evidence.text_span: These sequential synthetic transformations efficiently converted olefins 11c & 11d into the corresponding natural products N-Me-carbazoquinocin E 5 and N-Me-carbazoquinocin F 6, respectively (Scheme 3).

thought.background: The general strategy for synthesizing N-Me-carbazoquinocins B, D, and E has been established.
thought.gap: To synthesize N-Me-carbazoquinocin F, a different alkyl chain (iso-pentyl) needs to be introduced via Wittig olefination.
thought.decision: To perform Wittig olefination of aldehyde 2 using iso-pentylidene phosphorous ylide (9d), expecting the formation of Z-olefin (11d).

outcome.observation: Z-olefin (11d) was isolated in 80% yield as a pale-yellow gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: Wittig olefination of aldehyde 2 using iso-butylidene phosphorous ylide 9c (ref. 8) and iso-pentylidene phosphorous ylide 9d (ref. 8) exclusively afforded Z-olefins 11c (n = 0; 81% and 11d (n = 1; 80%), respectively.

thought.gap: The double bond needs to be reduced to a saturated alkyl chain.
thought.decision: To perform hydrogenation of 11d using Pd/C under a hydrogen atmosphere, expecting the formation of 12d.

outcome.observation: Hydrogenation product 12d was obtained in 95.4% yield as a colorless gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: After having both olefins 11c and 11d in hand, we employed the five-step synthetic strategy (as shown for N-Me-carbazoquinocin B 3 and D 4 in Scheme 2), i.e., hydrogenation (12c & 12d)

thought.gap: A bromine atom needs to be introduced at the C3 position for further functionalization.
thought.decision: To perform electrophilic bromination of 12d with NBS in CHCl3, expecting the formation of 13d.

outcome.observation: Bromination product 13d was obtained in 93% yield as a gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: After having both olefins 11c and 11d in hand, we employed the five-step synthetic strategy (as shown for N-Me-carbazoquinocin B 3 and D 4 in Scheme 2), i.e., hydrogenation (12c & 12d)–bromination (13c & 13d)

thought.gap: The bromine needs to be replaced by a methoxy group.
thought.decision: To perform methoxylation of 13d using NaOMe and CuI in DMF, expecting the formation of 14d.

outcome.observation: 3-methoxycarbazole 14d was obtained in 91% yield as a colorless liquid.

evidence.text_span: After having both olefins 11c and 11d in hand, we employed the five-step synthetic strategy (as shown for N-Me-carbazoquinocin B 3 and D 4 in Scheme 2), i.e., hydrogenation (12c & 12d)–bromination (13c & 13d)–methoxylation (14c & 14d)

thought.gap: The methoxy group needs to be deprotected to a hydroxyl group.
thought.decision: To perform demethylation of 14d using BBr3 in CH2Cl2, expecting the formation of phenol 15d.

outcome.observation: Phenol 15d was obtained in 93% yield as a gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: After having both olefins 11c and 11d in hand, we employed the five-step synthetic strategy (as shown for N-Me-carbazoquinocin B 3 and D 4 in Scheme 2), i.e., hydrogenation (12c & 12d)–bromination (13c & 13d)–methoxylation (14c & 14d)–demethylation (15c & 15d)

thought.gap: The final step is to oxidize the phenol to the carbazoquinone.
thought.decision: To perform oxidation of 15d using (PhSeO)2O in THF, expecting the formation of N-Me-carbazoquinocin F (6).

outcome.observation: N-Me-carbazoquinocin F (6) was obtained in 94% yield as a dark-brown glittering solid.

evidence.text_span: These sequential synthetic transformations efficiently converted olefins 11c & 11d into the corresponding natural products N-Me-carbazoquinocin E 5 and N-Me-carbazoquinocin F 6, respectively (Scheme 3).

thought.background: The general strategy for synthesizing N-Me-carbazoquinocins B, D, E, and F has been established.
thought.gap: To synthesize N-Me-carbazoquinocin A, a different approach is needed to introduce the 3-methylpentyl alkyl chain at the C1 position.
thought.decision: To start with carbazole ester (16) and perform alpha-methylation followed by conversion of the carboxylate group into an ethyl group to generate the required 3-methylpentyl chain.

outcome.observation: Alpha-methyl ester (17) was obtained in 59% yield as a pale-yellow liquid.

evidence.text_span: Accordingly, the LDA-mediated enolate formation of ester 16 followed by quenching with MeI gave alpha-methyl ester (17) in 59% yield.

thought.gap: The ester group needs to be reduced to a primary alcohol.
thought.decision: To perform reduction of 17 using LiAlH4, expecting the formation of primary alcohol (18).

outcome.observation: Primary alcohol (18) was obtained in 93% yield as a colorless gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: The reduction of 17 with LiAlH4 followed by oxidation of the resultant primary alcohol (18) with Dess–Martin periodinane (DMP) gave aldehyde (19) in 84% overall yield for two steps.

thought.gap: The primary alcohol needs to be oxidized to an aldehyde.
thought.decision: To perform oxidation of 18 using Dess–Martin periodinane (DMP) in dichloromethane, expecting the formation of aldehyde (19).

outcome.observation: Aldehyde (19) was obtained in 91% yield as a colorless liquid.

evidence.text_span: The reduction of 17 with LiAlH4 followed by oxidation of the resultant primary alcohol (18) with Dess–Martin periodinane (DMP) gave aldehyde (19) in 84% overall yield for two steps.

thought.gap: A terminal alkene needs to be introduced via Wittig olefination.
thought.decision: To perform Wittig olefination of aldehyde 19 using methylidenephosphorous ylide (generated from methyl(triphenyl phosphonium) bromide and tBuOK), expecting the formation of olefin (20).

outcome.observation: Olefin (20) was obtained in 84% yield as a colorless gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: Further, olefination of aldehyde 19 using methylidenephosphorous ylide, followed by hydrogenation of the resultant olefin (20) gave 1-[3-methylpentyl] carbazole (21).

thought.gap: The terminal alkene needs to be reduced to a saturated alkyl chain.
thought.decision: To perform hydrogenation of 20 using Pd/C under a hydrogen atmosphere, expecting the formation of 1-[3-methylpentyl] carbazole (21).

outcome.observation: Hydrogenation product 21 was obtained in 96% yield as a colorless liquid.

evidence.text_span: Further, olefination of aldehyde 19 using methylidenephosphorous ylide, followed by hydrogenation of the resultant olefin (20) gave 1-[3-methylpentyl] carbazole (21).

thought.gap: A bromine atom needs to be introduced at the C3 position for further functionalization.
thought.decision: To perform electrophilic bromination of 21 with NBS in CHCl3, expecting the formation of 3-bromocarbazole (22).

outcome.observation: 3-bromocarbazole (22) was obtained in 88% yield as a colorless liquid.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, we employed a four-step synthetic strategy, which involved bromination (22)

thought.gap: The bromine needs to be replaced by a methoxy group.
thought.decision: To perform methoxylation of 22 using NaOMe and CuI in DMF, expecting the formation of 3-methoxycarbazole (23).

outcome.observation: 3-methoxycarbazole (23) was obtained in 86% yield as a pale-yellow liquid.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, we employed a four-step synthetic strategy, which involved bromination (22)–methoxylation (23)

thought.gap: The methoxy group needs to be deprotected to a hydroxyl group.
thought.decision: To perform demethylation of 23 using BBr3 in CH2Cl2, expecting the formation of phenol (24).

outcome.observation: Phenol (24) was obtained in 70% yield as a light-yellow gummy compound.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, we employed a four-step synthetic strategy, which involved bromination (22)–methoxylation (23)–demethylation (24)

thought.gap: The final step is to oxidize the phenol to the carbazoquinone.
thought.decision: To perform oxidation of 24 using (PhSeO)2O in THF, expecting the formation of N-Me-carbazoquinocin A (7).

outcome.observation: N-Me-carbazoquinocin A (7) was obtained in 90% yield as a dark-brown glittering solid.

evidence.text_span: These sequential synthetic transformations efficiently converted carbazole 21 into N-Me-carbazoquinocin A 7 with an overall yield of 48% for four steps.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for the synthesis of all five natural products starting from commercially available indole.

validation.significance: Developed an efficient and practically viable synthetic approach for the unified synthesis of 3,4-carbazoquinone-based carbazole alkaloids N-Me-carbazoquinocins A, B and D–F, building upon a previously established acid-catalyzed intramolecular benzannulation.

source.title: Integrated synthesis of 3,4-carbazoquinone alkaloids N-Me-carbazoquinocin A, B and D-F.
source.authors: Bommanaboina B, Roy D, Baire B.
